courses.title: System Dynamics & Control I
courses.summary: Feedback made quantitative: modeling, transfer functions, stability, root locus, frequency response, and the PID controller — the theory under every temperature loop and speed governor in the plant.
taught_from: Ogata-tradition control theory via MIT OCW 2.04A Systems and Controls (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Mechanical, thermal, and fluid systems reduced to ODEs. Analogies across domains made explicit. The model as a decision about what to ignore.
preview: Model a heated tank as first order: what are its gain and time constant physically?
What did your model deliberately ignore, and when does that bite?

lessons.scope: From ODE to transfer function; poles and zeros as the system's anatomy. Block-diagram algebra. The s-plane as a map of behavior.
preview: What do poles and zeros each shape in a response?
Reduce a feedback loop's block diagram to one transfer function: what's the formula?

lessons.scope: Time constants, damping ratio, natural frequency, and the step-response vocabulary: rise time, overshoot, settling. Reading system character from a trend chart. The response shapes every operator recognizes without naming.
preview: Estimate damping ratio from 25% overshoot — roughly what value?
Why does rise time trade against overshoot?

lessons.scope: Feedback's gifts: disturbance rejection and desensitization — and its price: potential instability. Error constants and system type. Why integral action exists.
preview: Why does feedback reject disturbances but risk instability?
Which system type removes steady-state error to a step?

lessons.scope: The stability question posed algebraically. Routh arrays and parameter ranges for stability. Gain limits computed before anything oscillates in the field.
preview: What question does Routh–Hurwitz answer without solving roots?
Find the gain range for stability: what does the array's first column do?

lessons.scope: Pole migration with gain drawn by rule. Design by pole placement on the locus. Seeing why more gain destabilizes — graphically, permanently.
preview: Why does increasing gain drive poles toward instability on a locus?
What do asymptotes of the root locus tell you?

lessons.scope: Sinusoidal steady state; magnitude and phase plots constructed from asymptotes. Gain and phase margins as robustness currency. The frequency view that unifies control and vibration analysis.
preview: Read gain and phase margin off a Bode plot: what does each protect?
Why does the frequency view unify control and vibration thinking?

lessons.scope: P, I, and D actions each derived from a need; tuning by Ziegler–Nichols and by informed iteration. Integral windup and derivative noise handled practically. The controller that runs 90% of industry.
preview: What does integral action remove and what does it risk?
Why does derivative action hate noisy sensors?

lessons.scope: Actuators, positioners, and valve characteristics inside the loop. Deadband, stiction, and hysteresis as control killers. Loop problems that masquerade as tuning problems.
preview: What do stiction and deadband do to a well-tuned loop?
Why can a valve problem masquerade as a tuning problem?

lessons.scope: Sampling in the loop; the PLC/DCS as a discrete controller. Scan-rate effects on stability margins. Enough discrete theory to respect the digital reality.
preview: What does sampling too slowly do to stability margins?
Why does a PLC PID need anti-windup on output limits?

lessons.scope: Speed, tension, and temperature loops from real machinery contexts — an extruder barrel and a winder analyzed. Loop interaction previewed. Control theory attached to hardware nameplates.
preview: In the extruder temperature case, what made the loop slow — process or tuning?
Which loop of the winder interacts with tension, and how?

courses.title: Instrumentation & Process Control
courses.summary: The control loop as plumbing and paperwork: P&ID literacy, transmitters and control valves, loop tuning in the field, and the safety instrumented systems that sit above regulation — EQUATE-style process-plant territory.

lessons.t: The process control loop end to end
lessons.scope: Sensor, transmitter, controller, and final element as one signal chain. Loop drawings versus P&IDs. Every element's failure mode inventoried.
preview: Trace a level loop end to end: name every element's failure mode.
Loop drawing versus P&ID: what does each give the troubleshooter?

lessons.scope: ISA-style symbols and tag grammar decoded — the identification letters, loop numbers, and line conventions that make a P&ID legible. Equipment, instruments, and interlocks traced through a real sheet. The drawing a process plant hands you on day one, read without flinching.
preview: Decode the tag FIC-201: what does each letter say, and what does the number point to?
How does a P&ID tell you a control valve's failure position, and why does it matter in a trip?
What distinguishes instrument signal lines from process lines on the drawing?

lessons.scope: Globe, ball, and butterfly bodies; inherent versus installed characteristic derived from the pressure-ratio argument. Cv sizing in outline; cavitation and flashing in valves. Positioners and their diagnostics.
preview: Inherent versus installed valve characteristic: what bends the curve?
Why does an equal-percentage valve suit most flow loops?

lessons.scope: Ranging, elevation/suppression, and impulse-line practice consolidated. Smart transmitter diagnostics used properly. Calibration versus validation on live plants.
preview: Elevation versus suppression in DP level: when does each apply?
Calibration versus validation on a live plant: what differs?

lessons.scope: Process identification from bump tests: gain, time constant, dead time. Lambda and reaction-curve tuning applied to level, flow, and temperature archetypes. Dead time as the real enemy, named.
preview: From a bump test, extract gain, time constant, and dead time.
Why is dead time the real enemy of tight control?

lessons.scope: Cascade, ratio, and feedforward — each derived from a disturbance argument. Split-range and override control where hardware demands it. Reading these structures off a P&ID.
preview: When does cascade control earn its complexity?
What disturbance does feedforward kill that feedback cannot?

lessons.scope: DCS versus PLC/SCADA delineated honestly. Controllers, I/O, and HMI stations in a process-plant architecture. Alarm management at plant scale — the rationalization discipline.
preview: DCS versus PLC/SCADA: what genuinely differs?
What does alarm rationalization remove and preserve?

lessons.scope: The protection layers above control; SIS separated from BPCS by design. SIL concepts and proof testing in outline. Trip logic read from cause-and-effect diagrams.
preview: Why must SIS be independent of the BPCS?
What does a proof test prove?

lessons.scope: pH, conductivity, and gas analyzers in outline; sample systems as the real failure point. Analyzer maintenance economics. Where analyzers gate product release.
preview: Why do sample systems fail more than analyzers?
What does analyzer dead time do to control?

lessons.scope: Calibration programs, drift trending, and smart diagnostics as condition monitoring for instruments. Instrument criticality ranking. The instrument technician–reliability engineer interface.
preview: What makes instrument drift a condition-monitoring signal?
Rank instrument criticality: which loops earn tight calibration intervals?

lessons.scope: For one control loop: P&ID extract, instrument datasheets, valve sizing sketch, tuning plan, and test procedure. The document set of a small management-of-change. Instrumentation practiced as engineering, not adjustment.
preview: In the loop package, why does the valve sizing sketch precede tuning?
What does the test procedure verify that commissioning often skips?

courses.title: PLC II & Industrial Networks
courses.summary: Beyond single-rack thinking: structured programming, analog and PID in the PLC, fieldbuses and industrial Ethernet, drives integration, and the networked architecture of a modern line.

lessons.scope: Function blocks, structured text, and sequential function charts beside ladder. Choosing a language per task. Code organization for machines with modes.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Pick a language per task: sequence, math block, interlock — and justify.
What does a function block encapsulate that ladder rungs scatter?

lessons.scope: Modes, states, and transitions designed before coding. Auto/manual/maintenance mode architecture. The pattern that makes complex machines debuggable.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Design states for auto/manual/maintenance: what transitions need guarding?
Why do state machines make faults recoverable?

lessons.t: Analog processing and PID in the PLC
lessons.scope: Scaling, filtering, and alarm limits on analog channels. The PLC PID instruction configured and tuned. Temperature and flow loops closed in logic.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Why filter an analog input, and what does over-filtering cost?
Configure PID in the PLC: which three parameters first?

lessons.scope: Why networks replaced point wiring; topology, addressing, and determinism. Modbus RTU/TCP as the lingua franca. Reading a network architecture drawing.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: What does determinism mean on a fieldbus?
Why did Modbus survive forty years?

lessons.scope: EtherNet/IP and PROFINET in outline; switches, VLANs, and segmentation for machine networks. Diagnostics from port LEDs to managed-switch counters. When the 'PLC fault' is a network fault.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: What do managed-switch diagnostics reveal that ping cannot?
Why segment machine networks from office networks?

lessons.scope: Drive parameters, control words, and status words over the network. Speed and torque references from logic. Commissioning a networked drive without folklore.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: What do control and status words exchange with a drive?
Commission a networked VFD: which parameters are non-negotiable?

lessons.scope: Arrays, structures, and recipe management for product changeovers. Batch parameters downloaded safely. Production data captured at source for OEE.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Why do recipes beat hand-entered setpoints at changeover?
What data should logic capture at the moment of a fault?

lessons.scope: Screen hierarchy and navigation designed around the operator's tasks, not the tag database. High-performance HMI principles: gray backgrounds, analog context, and alarms that stand out because nothing else shouts. Binding displays to PLC data and testing the interface against abnormal situations, not just the happy path.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Why do high-performance HMIs keep most of the screen gray?
What should an overview screen let an operator judge in five seconds?

lessons.scope: Alarm philosophy applied in logic: priority, deadband, and state-based suppression. First-out capture for trips. Designing the diagnostics you will want during the outage.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Design first-out capture for a trip chain: what does it answer?
Which alarms deserve priority 1, and what does that imply for the rest?

lessons.scope: Why plant networks are attacked and how they are segmented, patched, and backed up. Remote access done with adult supervision. The maintenance engineer's cyber hygiene responsibilities.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: Name three OT-security practices a maintenance engineer owns.
Why is remote vendor access both necessary and dangerous?

lessons.scope: Two PLC stations, one network, shared interlocks, one HMI: specify, program, and fault-test the pair. Handshake design under failure conditions. The systems view PLC I could not yet teach.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Automation and Control; supplementary video: RealPars
preview: In the two-station project, what handshake failure did you design against?
Why does the HMI show line state, not just device state?

courses.title: Maintenance Engineering Fundamentals
courses.summary: The discipline you came from, rebuilt as engineering: failure behavior over time, the P–F interval, maintenance strategy types, and the vocabulary — availability, MTBF, criticality — used precisely instead of loosely.
taught_from: NPTEL: Maintenance and reliability-related engineering courses (nptel.ac.in)
Practitioner curriculum structured against the SMRP CMRP body-of-knowledge domains (framework, not a textbook)

lessons.scope: Failure as a process, not an event: load versus capability over time. Failure modes, mechanisms, and causes distinguished rigorously. The language that makes failure discussable.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL maintenance courses
preview: Failure mode versus mechanism versus cause: sort a seized bearing into all three.
Why is 'load exceeded capability' the universal failure sentence?

lessons.t: Failure patterns and the P–F curve
lessons.scope: The six classic failure patterns and the uncomfortable evidence that most failures are not age-related. The P–F curve derived as a concept: potential failure, functional failure, and the inspection window between them. The single most consequential diagram in modern maintenance.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; RCM literature as surveyed in NPTEL reliability courses
preview: Classic studies found most failure modes are not age-related: what does that do to time-based overhauls?
Define P and F on the P–F curve, and state the rule for a legitimate inspection interval between them.
A defect is detectable ~3 months before functional failure: what is the longest defensible monitoring interval?

lessons.scope: Run-to-failure, time-based, condition-based, and proactive strategies — each legitimate somewhere. Strategy selection by failure pattern and consequence. Why 'more PM' is not a strategy.
preview: Match each strategy to the failure pattern it suits.
Why is run-to-failure a legitimate strategy and not neglect?

lessons.scope: MTBF, MTTR, availability, and utilization defined with equations and abused-examples. Inherent versus operational availability. Calculating them from real work-order data, including the data's lies.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL reliability courses
preview: Compute availability from MTBF 400 h and MTTR 8 h.
Inherent versus operational availability: what does the gap contain?

lessons.scope: Ranking equipment by consequence and likelihood; criticality matrices built honestly. The 10% of assets that deserve 60% of engineering attention. Criticality as the master key to every later prioritization.
preview: Build a criticality matrix: which axes, and who scores?
Why does criticality drive every later prioritization?

lessons.scope: PM tasks written against failure modes, not habits. Intervals from failure data and P–F logic. The PM review: deleting tasks as a legitimate outcome.
preview: Write a PM task against a specific failure mode — what makes it effective?
Why is deleting PMs sometimes the right outcome of a review?

lessons.scope: Lubrication regimes from journal-bearing theory; grease versus oil decisions. Selection, storage, and contamination control. Lubrication as the cheapest reliability program in the plant.
preview: Which lubrication regime protects at startup, and why is startup the danger?
Grease versus oil: name two deciding factors.

lessons.scope: Alignment, balancing, fastener torque, and installation standards as failure-prevention engineering. The craftsmanship–reliability link quantified where possible. Why 'fitted and ran' is not a completion standard.
preview: Why does precision alignment extend seal and bearing life?
What does 'fitted and ran' fail to verify?

lessons.scope: CLAIR-style operator tasks: clean, lubricate, adjust, inspect, report. The operations–maintenance interface designed, not fought over. TPM origins acknowledged without the cult.
preview: Which operator tasks genuinely prevent failures?
Why does the operations–maintenance interface need design, not goodwill?

lessons.scope: Cost of downtime versus cost of maintenance; direct, indirect, and consequence costs. The optimum that moves with criticality. Making a maintenance budget argument management can audit.
preview: Which downtime costs never appear on the maintenance budget?
Why does the maintenance optimum move with criticality?

lessons.t: The maintenance function in the organization
lessons.scope: Roles: technician, planner, supervisor, reliability engineer — who owns what. Centralized versus area maintenance. Where the transitioning maintenance engineer's experience already maps onto this course's language.
preview: Map your current maintenance experience onto the roles in this lesson.
Which role owns strategy, and which owns this week's schedule?

courses.title: Reliability Engineering I
courses.summary: Quantified failure: reliability mathematics, Weibull analysis of real data, system modeling, and FMEA/FMECA — the analytical core of the reliability engineer role this pathway targets.

lessons.scope: Reliability, CDF, PDF, and hazard rate defined and interrelated with derivations. The bathtub curve as hazard-rate biography. Reading each function's story from the others.
preview: Relate R(t), F(t), f(t), and h(t): which pair determines the rest?
What does the bathtub curve's middle actually claim?

lessons.scope: Constant hazard rate and its memoryless consequence proven. MTBF's honest meaning under the exponential. Where the model applies — and the electronics-versus-wearout boundary.
preview: Prove memorylessness for the exponential in one line.
Why is MTBF alone a misleading purchase criterion?

lessons.scope: The Weibull distribution with shape and scale parameters read as physics: β<1 infant mortality, β≈1 random, β>1 wearout. Median-rank plotting of a real failure dataset, censored points handled correctly. The analysis that turns a failure log into a maintenance strategy argument.
preview: A seal population fits Weibull β = 0.7: what regime is that, and is scheduled replacement justified?
β = 3.2, η = 18 months: why plan intervention well before the MTBF, and roughly when?
Why must suspensions (censored units) enter the fit rather than being discarded?

lessons.scope: Data collection sins: mixed failure modes, missing suspensions, bad timestamps. Goodness of fit with judgment. Confidence bounds that keep conclusions honest.
preview: Name three data sins that corrupt Weibull fits.
What do confidence bounds add to a fitted line?

lessons.scope: Series, parallel, and k-out-of-n structures derived from probability first principles; reliability block diagrams of real equipment trains. Redundancy's diminishing returns and common-cause caveat. A pumping station modeled end to end as the condensed example.
preview: Two pumps in parallel, each R = 0.90, either sufficient: system reliability?
Three series components at R = 0.95 each: system reliability?
Why does adding redundancy do nothing against common-cause failures?

lessons.scope: Repairable-system measures; availability from failure and repair distributions. Maintainability as design property — access, modularity, error-proofing. Where MTTR is really spent, measured.
preview: Availability versus reliability: a machine with poor MTBF but great MTTR — what results?
Where does maintainability get designed in?

lessons.scope: Failure modes and effects analysis built properly: functions, functional failures, modes, effects, severity–occurrence–detection scoring, and the RPN's known abuses. Criticality analysis as the FMECA extension. One equipment FMEA worked as the condensed example — the template interviews ask you to narrate.
preview: Two failure modes both score RPN 72 — one from severity 9, one from occurrence 9. Why must they be treated differently?
For a leaking mechanical seal, give one failure mode, one cause, and one effect — correctly sorted.
What does a detection score of 9 actually assert about your monitoring capability?

lessons.scope: Top events, gates, and minimal cut sets; quantification with basic-event probabilities. FTA versus FMEA — deductive versus inductive. Protective-system failures as the classic application.
preview: Build a fault tree for 'pump fails to start': first two gates?
What does a minimal cut set expose?

lessons.scope: Reliability-centered maintenance's seven questions and its decision logic: condition-based first, time-based where age matters, run-to-failure where consequences permit, redesign where nothing works. Hidden failures and failure-finding intervals. The framework that disciplines every PM program worth the name.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Reliability Engineering; SMRP CMRP framework
preview: Why does RCM route hidden failures to failure-finding tasks, and what makes a failure 'hidden'?
Under what two conditions does RCM legitimately assign run-to-failure?
Explain how a blanket monthly-PM policy over- and under-maintains the same plant simultaneously.

lessons.scope: Maintenance-induced failures: the uncomfortable data. Error-provocative designs and procedures. Designing tasks so the night shift succeeds.
preview: Why do maintenance-induced failures spike after intrusive PMs?
Name two error-provocative designs you have met.

lessons.scope: From a provided failure log: clean, classify, Weibull-fit, model the system, and recommend strategy changes with quantified backing. The full Reliability I toolkit exercised once. Deliverable formatted as a plant engineering report.
preview: In the data project, what did cleaning change about the conclusion?
Which recommendation survived the confidence bounds?

courses.title: Corrosion & Degradation
courses.summary: The Gulf's tax on steel: electrochemical fundamentals, the corrosion forms bestiary, marine and coastal service, and the defenses — materials, coatings, cathodic protection, inhibitors — that asset integrity budgets buy.
taught_from: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 17; NPTEL: Corrosion-related materials courses (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Anodes, cathodes, and the corrosion cell derived from half-reactions. Galvanic series versus EMF series — the practical one wins. Corrosion rate units and conversions.
preview: Identify anode and cathode in a rusting couple — where does metal leave?
Why does the galvanic series beat the EMF series for practice?

lessons.scope: Chlorides, heat, humidity, and marine atmosphere as the region's standing corrosion load. Pitting and crevice attack on stainless; CUI — corrosion under insulation — as the hidden budget-eater; seawater cooling circuits. The condensed example: ranking degradation threats for a coastal plant's asset register.
preview: Why do chlorides defeat stainless passivity, and which two localized attack forms result?
Corrosion under insulation: why does insulation make attack worse, and what keeps it hidden from surveys?
For a coastal Gulf plant, name three chloride-driven threats you would register before any inspection data exists.

lessons.scope: The 'benign' uniform mode and when it isn't; galvanic couples with area-ratio arithmetic; crevices as chemistry chambers. Design details that create or kill crevices. Gasket and fastener implications.
preview: Why does a small anode with a big cathode corrode catastrophically?
What makes a crevice its own chemistry chamber?

lessons.scope: Pit initiation on passive films; chloride's special villainy. PREN as a stainless selection number. Why pits sink ships that thickness surveys called healthy.
preview: Why do pits sink equipment that thickness surveys called healthy?
What does PREN rank, and what does it ignore?

lessons.scope: Stress corrosion cracking's three-ingredient recipe; chloride SCC of austenitics; hydrogen embrittlement and sulfide stress cracking in outline. Why EAC failures are sudden and forensic. Materials–environment pairings memorized by the industry.
preview: Name SCC's three ingredients and one industrial pairing.
Why are EAC failures sudden rather than gradual?

lessons.scope: Erosion-corrosion, cavitation damage, and fretting. Velocity limits in piping specs explained. The pump-impeller damage gallery interpreted.
preview: Erosion-corrosion versus cavitation damage: how do the surfaces differ?
Why do velocity limits appear in piping specs?

lessons.scope: Oxidation kinetics and scale protectiveness; sulfidation and fuel-ash attack in outline. Creep–corrosion interactions at furnace conditions. The melt-shop and fired-heater materials problem.
preview: What makes an oxide scale protective versus useless?
Where does creep meet corrosion in a fired heater?

lessons.scope: The selection ladder: carbon steel with corrosion allowance, coated steel, 304 and 316 stainless, duplex grades, and nickel alloys — each step priced against the environment it must survive. PREN put to work; galvanizing and aluminizing as sacrificial armor. Nonmetallics — GRP and HDPE — where metals lose the argument.
preview: What does 316's molybdenum buy over 304 in chloride service?
When does a nonmetallic pipe beat every alloy on the ladder?

lessons.scope: Surface preparation standards as the real quality variable; coating families and their service envelopes. Inspection: holiday testing and DFT. Coating failure modes read like weld defects.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 17; NPTEL corrosion content
preview: Why does surface preparation outweigh coating choice?
What does holiday testing find?

lessons.scope: Sacrificial and impressed-current CP with the electrochemistry made explicit. CP surveys in outline. Inhibitors and chemical treatment where coatings can't go.
preview: Sacrificial versus impressed-current CP: when does each fit?
What does a CP survey actually measure?

lessons.scope: Coupons, ER probes, and thickness-monitoring locations; corrosion rates fed into remaining-life arithmetic. Corrosion management systems as documents that direct money. The bridge into Year 4's asset integrity course.
preview: Where do you place corrosion coupons for a truthful rate?
Turn a corrosion rate into remaining life: what arithmetic and what caveats?
